patches.hypothesis: The issue arises from java.util.Date not handling BC dates properly due to its structure, which doesn't store the era information. Thus, conversions of BC dates incorrectly assume AD. We need to manually handle BC years.

patches.hypothesis: The method incorrectly handles dates in the BC (before Christ) era, assuming all years are AD (anno Domini). It needs to account for the ERA field correctly.

+    era == GregorianCalendar.BC ? -(date.getYear() + 1900 - 1) : date.getYear() + 1900
patches.diff:

+    era == GregorianCalendar.BC ? -(date.getYear() + 1900 - 1) : date.getYear() + 1900

patches.hypothesis: The original patch partially handled the conversion of a Date to LocalDateTime by properly addressing BCE years, but it relied on a mix of deprecated Date methods and incorrect assumptions. The refined patch uses GregorianCalendar to accurately extract all relevant components, thus ensuring correct handling of edge cases such as leap years and daylight saving time transitions, without relying on obsolete Date methods.
patches.original_hypothesis: The method does not correctly handle years in BC era from the java.util.Date, leading to incorrect year calculations.

patches.hypothesis: The method doesn't handle BCE years correctly, as it directly takes the year from Calendar without considering the ERA field.
patches.changes: - Handle the ERA field from calendar to adjust the year correctly for BCE dates.
patches.diff: - Handle the ERA field from calendar to adjust the year correctly for BCE dates.
patches.original_code:
//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The method does not account for the BC era when extracting the year from the calendar, causing incorrect LocalDateTime creation.
patches.changes: + int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); + if (calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.BC) { + yearOfEra = -(yearOfEra - 1); + }
patches.diff: + int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); + if (calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.BC) { + yearOfEra = -(yearOfEra - 1); + }
patches.original_code:
//----------------------------------------------------------------------- /** * Constructs a LocalDateTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalDateTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local date, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalDateTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }

patches.hypothesis: The original method does not account for the BC era, which causes incorrect year calculation. The year needs adjustment based on the calendar era.
patches.changes: + int year = (calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.BC) ? 1 - yearOfEra : yearOfEra;
patches.diff: + int year = (calendar.get(Calendar.ERA) == GregorianCalendar.BC) ? 1 - yearOfEra : yearOfEra;

It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from, not null * @return the created local date-time, not null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */ public static LocalDateTime fromCalendarFields(Calendar calendar) { if (calendar == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("The calendar must not be null"); } int yearOfEra = calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR); return new LocalDateTime( yearOfEra, calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1, calendar.get(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH), calendar.get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY), calendar.get(Calendar.MINUTE), calendar.get(Calendar.SECOND), calendar.get(Calendar.MILLISECOND) ); }
